one_line_takeaway: A self-contained MATLAB/Simulink virtual 10-DG microgrid plus LightGBM detectors that hit 99.72% multiclass / 94.8% binary accuracy on synthetic secondary-control attack data, with knowledge distillation cutting model size 87.7% and inference ~73% for CPU-only edge deployment, but with no external baseline and only self-generated data.
key_finding: The multiclass LightGBM classifier reached 99.72% accuracy / 99.62% macro-F1 (99.72% weighted-F1), and the binary detector 94.8% accuracy / 94.3% F1 with very low false-negative rate (attacks caught >99% of cases; most errors are false positives on normal samples). Knowledge distillation shrank the multiclass model from 4.83 MB to 0.60 MB (87.7% reduction) and cut inference time ~73% (student ~18.0 ms/1000 samples vs teacher 67.0 ms/1000) while keeping 99.70% accuracy/F1. All models meet the paper's stated real-time budget (20-50 ms detection window, 100-200 ms secondary-control update interval) on CPU only. Ablation shows the three bus-voltage features (V1-V3) are the single most informative group (removing them drops macro-F1 by ~1.01 pt, 0.9948 to 0.9847), while active/reactive power, frequency, and line-current groups are individually redundant (0-0.01% drop). Normal operation is the hardest class (F1 ~0.983); all six attack classes exceed 0.996.
core_contribution: The authors build a complete, self-contained pipeline for detecting cyberattacks on the distributed secondary-control layer of inverter-based AC microgrids. Their own contributions (as distinct from the PSERC S-82G resiliency model [2] and Rath et al.'s cyber-secure distributed control [7], which they extend and cite as prior structure) are: (1) a high-fidelity 10-DG virtual AC microgrid in MATLAB/Simulink simulated at a 2 microsecond fixed switching step; (2) a structured attack-injection framework (via MGLib) that corrupts the consensus correction signals xi (frequency) and zeta_i (voltage) directly at the secondary-control interface; (3) self-generated labeled datasets across seven operating modes (Normal plus six attacks); and (4) lightweight LightGBM binary and multiclass detectors plus a knowledge-distilled student model targeting CPU-only/embedded microgrid controllers. The stated novelty is combining high-fidelity cyber-physical simulation, structured secondary-control attack paths, and boosted-tree + knowledge-distillation models for real-time multiclass detection.
threat_model: An adversary who compromises a single low-bandwidth peer-to-peer communication link in the ring-topology secondary-control network of a 10-DG microgrid. Because DGs share consensus signals only with neighbors, corrupting the incoming signal at one node (they inject at DG1) propagates across multiple DGs. The attacker injects malicious modifications into the consensus correction signals xi (distorting active power/frequency regulation), zeta_i (distorting reactive power/voltage regulation), or both (coordinated). Six attack primitives are modeled: additive bias (constant offset, classical FDI), ramp (growing bias on xi), slow ramp (very small slope on zeta_i for stealth), sinusoidal (oscillatory injection on both), coordinated stealth (small correlated deviations kept within normal envelopes across multiple DGs), and denial-of-service (freezing/holding stale consensus data). Sensors and inverter hardware are not directly attacked; disturbances propagate through the cyber-control channel into physical V/I/P/Q/f measurements. The defender is a supervised classifier consuming the physical measurement stream.
limitations: The evaluation is entirely on the authors' own synthetic Simulink data from one 10-DG topology; no real hardware, field data, or external dataset is used, so generalization is unproven. There is no comparison against any external baseline detector run on the same data (CNN/LSTM/random-forest/XGBoost are only discussed conceptually, never trained/benchmarked here); all reported comparisons are internal (binary vs multiclass vs distilled, plus feature ablation). The attacker is only ever injected at DG1 in a single ring topology, and the near-perfect multiclass numbers on self-generated, cleanly-labeled data raise concerns about optimistic separability and possible temporal leakage from random-sample (rather than time-blocked) splitting of a downsampled continuous time series. The feature-importance narrative (active power and line currents dominant) is in mild tension with the ablation (voltage most informative, power/current redundant), which is not reconciled. Robustness is limited to inherent switching/discretization/jitter noise; no adaptive or adaptive-evasion adversary is tested. KD latency/size claims are measured on a virtualized Colab CPU, not on the claimed target embedded hardware (ARM Cortex-M, TI DSP, Raspberry Pi).

datasets_used: Self-generated MATLAB/Simulink virtual 10-DG AC microgrid dataset: 7 operating modes (Normal, Additive, Ramp, Slow Ramp, Sinusoid, Stealth, DoS), 500,001 samples/scenario at 2 us step over 1 s, 38 continuous features + binary and multiclass labels, ~3.5M total samples
Downsampled working dataset ~1,030,197 samples (all attack samples kept, 10-15% of normal retained)
quantitative_results: Multiclass LightGBM: 99.72% accuracy, 99.62% macro-F1, 99.72% weighted-F1, 67.00 ms per 1000 samples inference
Binary LightGBM: 94.8% accuracy, 94.3% macro-F1, 94.3% weighted-F1, 53.96 ms per 1000 samples
Distilled student (KD): 99.70% accuracy, 99.70% macro-F1/weighted-F1, ~18.0 ms per 1000 samples
Model size: multiclass 4.83 MB to student 0.60 MB (87.7% reduction); inference time reduced ~73% by KD
Real-time processing delay ~54-67 ms per 1000 samples on CPU-only Colab Intel Xeon 2.20 GHz
Ablation baseline macro-F1 0.9948; removing V1-V3 bus voltage drops to 0.9847 (1.01% drop, most informative group); removing PDG/QDG/fDG/I1-I3 each 0-0.01% drop
Per-class F1: Normal ~0.983 (hardest); all six attack classes >0.996
baselines_compared: No external detector was trained or benchmarked; CNN/LSTM/transformer, random forest [10], and XGBoost [14] are discussed from the literature only, not run on this data
Internal comparisons only: binary vs multiclass vs knowledge-distilled student LightGBM (Table II)
Feature-group ablation (Table IV) removing active power, reactive power, frequency, bus voltage, and line-current groups
